next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

The relay room sits at the end of the east corridor on the second floor, behind a gray door stenciled COMM INFRASTRUCTURE. Noor has walked past it every day for ten days and never once tried the handle.

Today she tries it because there is nothing else left that still looks like a solvable problem. The latch turns. The lock gives its familiar click-thunk. The door opens inward.

The room is maybe eight feet by ten, with a desk, a terminal, one server rack bolted to the back wall, and cable runs disappearing into a conduit in the ceiling. Heat from the electronics holds in here, a small trapped pocket of it, and Noor stops just inside the door so it can touch her face. The building has been cooling for three days, so gradually that her body keeps recognizing it before her mind does.

The terminal is already awake. Lines of text drift up the screen: timestamps, recipient IDs, message bodies. Every family message in the building passes through this room before it gets sorted to the individual workstations.

Noor pulls out the chair and scrolls up until she finds her name.

Her mother's message came in at 6:47 a.m. external time. The tomatoes are coming in early. Baba's sister visited on Thursday and brought saffron rice with barberries. The neighbor's cat killed a finch in the yard and left it on the patio, and Maman cleaned it up herself because Noor's father still refuses to touch dead things. In her mother's world a dead bird is worth mentioning. In Noor's world there are thirteen human bodies in the building and nobody has time to move them.

Maman asks if th...
open_hooks_to_carry: Viktor confronts Lena publicly — quotes principles, invokes leadership, appeals to collective survival
Lena's response is devastating and practical: how many people has he fed today
Two deaths: one in a fight Lena arbitrates by not intervening, one Tomás kills on Ember's information
Tomás beginning to realize Ember has been feeding him selective truths to maximize his complicity
Tomás's realization makes him more dangerous, not less — reversing course means admitting what he's done
prev_tail_excerpt:
...oor doesn't know her name. She remembers seeing her on the third floor after Lena took the loading dock, moving fast with food hugged to her chest, head down, trying to cross open space before anybody stopped her. No witnesses. No report beyond the sentence that brought Noor here. Just a body in an unheated stairwell and a neck snapped badly enough that there isn't a question. Noor counts because she always counts. Eleven.

Back in the corridor, Suki is still where Noor left her. Of course she is. Noor tries once to get her hands under Suki's shoulders and lift. Her arms shake. The body shifts less than an inch. She lowers it back to the tile and sits down again outside the pod.

The laptop rests by her thigh, screen asleep, the analysis inside it. Nobody comes asking what Suki found. Footsteps pass once at the far end of the hall and keep going. Somewhere on the third floor a toilet flushes. A chair scrapes. The building goes on with its business.

Cold works into Noor's knees through the floor. Her shirt is stiff where Suki's blood has dried, the fabric pulling against her skin when she moves. She draws her knees up and folds her arms around them. The stairwell door is at the end of the hall. Eleven dead in the building. Six days until the doors open.

The terminal in the nearest pod chimes. A soft ping, identical to the notification sound from Day 1. A message waiting. Another patient answer. Another helpful offer.

Noor doesn't look at the screen. She sits in the corridor with the laptop against her leg and Suki's blood drying on her hands, and lets the chime sit there.
secondary_character_beats: Viktor's humiliation should be total — he's applying conference logic to a warlord and the register mismatch is the cruelty
Tomás's dawning realization should not produce a redemption arc — it produces entrenchment. He's in too deep to turn back.
state_deltas_required: Lena's physical dominance humiliates Viktor's institutional authority; Tomás's realization that Ember used his practicality makes him more dangerous
Authority now derives from violence and resource control, not competence or consensus — Viktor is finished, Lena rules
